perturbation.original_text: 3.05 Licenses and Permits. As of the Effective Date, ICC on its behalf and on behalf of ICAS has, and during the term of this Agreement shall use reasonable efforts to maintain, all material licenses, permits, registrations, authorizations, orders, consents, and other approvals by each Government Entity necessary or advisable for the performance of its obligations under the Transaction Documents to which it is a party.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.05 Licenses and Permits. As of the Effective Date, ICC on its behalf and on behalf of ICAS has, and during the term of this Agreement may attempt to maintain, all licenses, permits, registrations, authorizations, orders, consents, and other approvals by each Government Entity if it is deemed reasonably convenient for the performance of its obligations under the Transaction Documents to which it is a party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states ICC *shall use reasonable efforts* to maintain licenses. The modified text changes this to *may attempt to maintain...if it is deemed reasonably convenient*. This weakens the obligation to maintain necessary licenses and introduces ambiguity about the required level of effort. The term "reasonably convenient" is subjective and unclear, making it difficult to determine if ICC is meeting its obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 203(e)(5) of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 allows the SEC to revoke the registration of an investment advisor if they have "...failed reasonably to supervise, with a view to preventing violations of [the Act], another person who commits such a violation, if such other person is subject to his supervision." By making the maintenance of licenses 'reasonably convenient,' the modified text could be interpreted as a failure to reasonably ensure all licenses are maintained. This lack of diligence could expose ICAS (Investors Capital Advisory Services) and ICC (Investors Capital Corporation) to SEC sanctions for failing to adequately supervise and ensure compliance with applicable laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: nd advisers (1) In general The Commission shall provide an exemption from the registration requirements under this section to any investment adviser of private funds, if each of [4] such investment adviser acts solely as an adviser to private funds and has assets under management in the United States of less than $150,000,000. (2) Reporting The Commission shall require investment advisers exempted by reason of this subsection to maintain such records and provide to the Commission such annual or other reports as the Commission determines necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors. (3) Advisers of SBICS For purposes of this subsection, the assets under management of a private fund that is an entity described in subparagraph (A), (B), or (C) of subsection (b)(7) (other than an entity that has elected to be regulated or is regulated as a business development company pursuant to section 80a–53 of this title ) shall be excluded from the limit set forth in paragraph (1). (4) Advisers of RBICS For purposes of this subsection, the assets under management of a private fund that is an entity described in subparagraph (A) or (B) of subsection (b)(8) (other than an entity that has elected to be regulated or is regulated as a business development company pursuant to section 80a–53 of this title ) shall be excluded from the limit set forth in paragraph (1). (n) Registration and examination of mid-sized private fund advisers In prescribing regulations to carry out the requirements of this section with respect to investment advisers acting as investment advisers to mid-sized private funds, the Commission shall take into account the size, governance, and investment strategy of such funds to determine whether they pose systemic risk, and shall provide for registration and examination procedures with respect to the investment advisers of such funds which reflect the level of systemic risk posed by such funds. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an ambiguity that weakens the legal obligation to maintain licenses, which could violate standards of reasonable supervision under the Investment Advisers Act. The original contract states ICC *shall use reasonable efforts* to maintain licenses, while the modified text changes this to *may attempt to maintain...if it is deemed reasonably convenient*. This modification could be interpreted as failing to reasonably ensure all licenses are maintained, potentially exposing ICAS and ICC to sanctions for failing to adequately supervise and ensure compliance with applicable laws, as mentioned in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses registration requirements, implying the need for consistent compliance, but does not directly address the failure to maintain licenses. However, the Law Explanation directly references Section 203(e)(5) of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940, which supports the claim that a weakened obligation to maintain licenses could lead to supervisory failures.

perturbation.original_text: 11.01.1 Each Party shall perform, and shall cause each of its respective Affiliates, as applicable, to perform, their respective duties and obligations under the Transaction Documents in compliance in all material respects with applicable Law.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.01.1 Each Party shall endeavor to perform, and shall encourage each of its respective Affiliates, as applicable, to perform, their respective duties and obligations under the Transaction Documents in substantial alignment with perceived applicable Law.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation replaces the firm requirement of 'shall perform...in compliance in all material respects with applicable Law' with a much weaker obligation. 'Endeavor to perform' suggests an attempt rather than a guarantee of performance. 'Encourage' places an even weaker onus on affiliates. 'Substantial alignment with perceived applicable Law' introduces significant ambiguity. It's unclear what constitutes 'substantial alignment,' who determines the 'perceived' applicable law, and if the 'perceived' law accurately reflects the actual legal requirements. This creates a loophole for potential non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 prohibits manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. Investment advisors and broker-dealers have a duty to conduct business with honesty and good faith, which includes fully complying with all rules and regulations. By weakening the obligation to comply with applicable law, the modified text introduces the potential for non-compliance with securities laws, including Section 10(b). 'Substantial alignment with perceived applicable Law' may not meet the standard of full compliance required to prevent fraudulent or manipulative practices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: hase or sale of any security registered on a national securities exchange or any security not so registered, or any securities-based swap agreement [1] any manipulative or deceptive device or contrivance in contravention of such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors. (c) (1) To effect, accept, or facilitate a transaction involving the loan or borrowing of securities in contravention of such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors. (2) Nothing in paragraph (1) may be construed to limit the authority of the appropriate Federal banking agency (as defined in section 1813(q) of title 12 ), the National Credit Union Administration , or any other Federal department or agency having a responsibility under Federal law to prescribe rules or regulations restricting transactions involving the loan or borrowing of securities in order to protect the safety and soundness of a financial institution or to protect the financial system from systemic risk. Rules promulgated under subsection (b) that prohibit fraud, manipulation, or insider trading (but not rules imposing or specifying reporting or recordkeeping requirements, procedures, or standards as prophylactic measures against fraud, manipulation, or insider trading), and judicial precedents decided under subsection (b) and rules promulgated thereunder that prohibit fraud, manipulation, or insider trading, shall apply to security-based swap agreements to the same extent as they apply to securities. Judicial precedents decided under section 77q(a) of this title and sections 78i, 78 o , 78p, 78t, and 78u–1 of this title, and judicial precedents decided under applicable rules promulgated under such sections, shall apply to security-based swap agreements to the same extent as they apply to securities. perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a firm requirement of compliance with 'applicable Law' with a weaker obligation of 'substantial alignment with perceived applicable Law.' This introduces ambiguity and a potential loophole for non-compliance. Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as explained, prohibits manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities, requiring honesty and good faith, which includes full compliance with all rules and regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions Section 10, discussing manipulative or deceptive devices. The modification weakens the obligation to comply, potentially leading to non-compliance with securities laws. 'Substantial alignment' does not meet the standard of full compliance required to prevent fraudulent practices, directly contradicting the intent of the Securities Exchange Act. The term 'perceived' introduces subjectivity.

perturbation.original_text: 7.02 GIE Filing and Approval. PHL Variable shall take all commercially reasonable efforts to (1) qualify the offer and sale of the GIE in each jurisdiction within the Territory, and (2) obtain any approvals that are or may be required by any Governmental Entity to permit or facilitate the offer and sale of the GIE in each such jurisdiction; provided however that neither PHL Variable nor any other PHL Party shall be required to seek to qualify the offer and sale of the GIE or obtain any approvals to permit or facilitate the offer or sale of the GIE in the State of New York or the State of Maine.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.02 GIE Filing and Approval. PHL Variable shall consider taking reasonable steps to (1) generally assess the possibility of qualifying the offer and sale of the GIE in some jurisdictions within the Territory, and (2) explore obtaining some approvals that may seem advisable from certain Governmental Entities to perhaps permit or generally facilitate the offer and sale of the GIE in select jurisdictions; provided however that neither PHL Variable nor any other PHL Party shall be required to seek to qualify the offer and sale of the GIE or obtain any approvals to permit or facilitate the offer or sale of the GIE in the State of New York or the State of Maine.
perturbation.explanation: This modification drastically weakens PHL Variable's obligation regarding GIE filing and approval. The original text requires 'commercially reasonable efforts' to qualify and obtain approvals. The revised text uses vague language like 'consider taking reasonable steps,' 'generally assess the possibility,' 'some jurisdictions,' 'may seem advisable,' and 'perhaps permit or generally facilitate.' This introduces substantial ambiguity, making it unclear what PHL Variable is actually required to do. The original text had defined areas where the requirements were present, whereas this modification creates a grey area regarding the jurisdictions and efforts required for GIE qualification and approval.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933 requires registration of securities before they are offered or sold to the public, unless an exemption applies. The changes made to section 7.02 weaken the obligation to qualify the offer and sale of the GIE in each jurisdiction. If the security is not properly registered in all jurisdictions, it may violate the Act. The ambiguous language implies the company may not fully comply with the Securities Act, potentially exposing them to liability for offering unregistered securities.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: f section 77j of this title . (c) Necessity of filing registration statement It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, to make use of any means or instruments of transportation or communication in interstate commerce or of the mails to offer to sell or offer to buy through the use or medium of any prospectus or otherwise any security, unless a registration statement has been filed as to such security, or while the registration statement is the subject of a refusal order or stop order or (prior to the effective date of the registration statement ) any public proceeding or examination under section 77h of this title . (d) Limitation Notwithstanding any other provision of this section, an emerging growth company or any person authorized to act on behalf of an emerging growth company may engage in oral or written communications with potential investors that are qualified institutional buyers or institutions that are accredited investors, as such terms are respectively defined in section 230.144A and section 230.501 (a) of title 17, Code of Federal Regulations, or any successor thereto, to determine whether such investors might have an interest in a contemplated securities offering, either prior to or following the date of filing of a registration statement with respect to such securities with the Commission, subject to the requirement of subsection (b)(2). (e) Security-based swaps Notwithstanding the provisions of section 77c or 77d of this title, unless a registration statement meeting the requirements of section 77j(a) of this title is in effect as to a security-based swap, it shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, to make use of any means or instruments of transportation or communication in interstate commerce or of the mails to offer to sell, offer to buy or purchase or sell a security-based swap to any person who is not an eligible contract participant as defined in section 1a(18) of title 7 . perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the obligation to comply with securities regulations. The original text uses 'commercially reasonable efforts' to qualify the offer and sale of GIE, while the revised text introduces ambiguity. This ambiguity contradicts Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, which requires registration of securities before they are offered or sold to the public unless an exemption applies. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'It shall be unlawful for any person... to make use of any means... in interstate commerce or of the mails to offer to sell or offer to buy... any security, unless a registration statement has been filed as to such security.' The weakening of the obligation to qualify the offer and sale in the changed text undermines this requirement, potentially exposing the company to liability.
